title: Iran Targets Strait of Hormuz's Digital Chokepoint as US Pours Billions into Counter-Drone Tech
subhead: Iran's threats against undersea internet cables expose a critical vulnerability in global digital infrastructure, while AI-powered drones reshape both offensive and defensive warfare in Ukraine and the Middle East. Meanwhile, German investigators uncover a major sanctions-evasion network routing dual-use technology to Russia through Turkey.

sections.heading: Iran Threatens Undersea Cables in Strait of Hormuz
sections.body:
Iranian state-linked media has floated plans to charge US technology companies including Meta, Google, Amazon, and Microsoft licensing fees for undersea internet cables passing through the Strait of Hormuz. Military spokesperson Ebrahim Zolfaghari posted bluntly: "We will impose fees on internet cables."

The threat targets five major submarine cable systems transiting the narrow waterway, including Asia-Africa-Europe-1 (AAE-1) and FALCON, which connect Gulf states to global networks. The Strait is just 21 nautical miles wide at its narrowest point, creating what analysts call "one of the world's most critical digital bottlenecks."

The timing is not accidental. The US-Iran war has already halted new cable projects in the region. French company Alcatel Submarine Networks suspended work on a Meta-backed cable project after one of its ships became stranded near Saudi Arabia in March. Cable repair ships have avoided the Strait since hostilities began, leaving potential faults unrepaired.

Iranian proposals would claim exclusive rights to repair and maintain cables in Iranian territorial waters, while charging annual licensing fees to US tech giants. The FALCON and Gulf Bridge International cables run partially through Iranian waters at certain points.

While less than 1% of global international bandwidth transits Hormuz, Middle Eastern and Asian economies face disproportionate exposure. A 24-hour disruption in India could cost its IT economy an estimated $920 million. Gulf states with limited cable diversity—Qatar, Bahrain, and Kuwait—would face immediate connectivity crises.
sections.whyItMatters: Digital infrastructure has become leverage in state conflict. Iran is signaling it can weaponize control over submarine cables just as it has threatened oil transit through the Strait, expanding the domain of coercive pressure from energy to data.

sections.heading: US Awards $500M Counter-Drone Contract as Iran War Accelerates Demand
sections.body:
The Pentagon awarded California-based startup Perennial Autonomy a $500 million contract to accelerate procurement of AI-enabled counter-drone systems, including Merops interceptors, Bumblebee quadcopters, and Hornet midrange strike drones.

The decision came from the Joint Interagency Task Force 401 (JIATF-401), created to research, test, and procure counter-UAS technology. Army Brig. Gen. Matt Ross called drones "the defining threat of our time" and emphasized the need for "layered defense" with low-cost, attritable interceptors.

The contract validates technology developed for Ukraine. Perennial, originally launched as Project Eagle by former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, created the Merops interceptor to counter Russian Shahed drones. The same systems are now being deployed against Iranian drones targeting US forces.

The cost calculus matters: each Merops interceptor costs approximately $15,000, while Shahed drones run $30,000-$50,000. Army Secretary Daniel Driscoll described a war of attrition where the economics favor the defender.

Perennial has also opened manufacturing operations in Europe through partnerships aimed at scaling Merops production. The company previously received a $5.2 million contract for the Bumblebee V2 system in January.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran war has transformed counter-drone technology from a niche requirement into a core defense priority. Ukraine's drone innovations are being rapidly absorbed into US military procurement as AI-enabled autonomous systems become essential infrastructure defense.

sections.heading: Ukraine's AI 'Slaughterbot' Drones Hunt Russian Soldiers
sections.body:
Russian military bloggers and Telegram channels are circulating reports of a new Ukrainian FPV drone equipped with thermal imaging and AI guidance that detects targets' faces and fires explosively formed projectiles (EFPs) at their heads. Video footage shows a Russian soldier apparently cut down by a precise head shot from such a drone.

The drones reportedly use low-cost TFL-1 AI autonomy modules—priced at around $442 per unit—developed by companies like Auterion and The Fourth Law. These add-on modules convert standard FPV drones into smart munitions with terminal guidance that can pursue targets even under electronic jamming.

AI-enabled FPVs claim around an 80% hit rate versus 40% for manually controlled drones. The EFP warheads, detonated at distance rather than on impact, can penetrate netting and wire cages designed to protect infantry.

Ukraine aims to produce 7 million FPVs this year. Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces commander Robert "Magyar" Brovdi has stated that a prime goal is eliminating Russian soldiers faster than they can be recruited—more than 30,000 per month. Recent drone kill figures suggest this threshold may have been reached.
sections.whyItMatters: Autonomous targeting systems have moved from experimental to operational. The barrier to AI-guided weapons has collapsed—$100 Raspberry Pi hardware can now enable facial recognition on cheap commercial drones, raising urgent questions about proliferation and control.

sections.heading: Germany Uncovers Russia Sanctions Evasion Network Using Turkey
sections.body:
German authorities arrested a 39-year-old businessman, identified as Nikita S., following a four-year investigation into a sanctions-evasion network that allegedly routed European dual-use technology to Russia's military industry through Turkish intermediaries.

The operation centered on Global Trade, a Lübeck-based trading firm that investigators say became a procurement arm for Moscow after the 2022 invasion. The firm allegedly moved roughly 16,000 shipments worth more than €30 million ($35 million).

The network used Turkish company MR Global as a transit hub, while Russian firm Kolovrat (also operating as Siderius) coordinated procurement from Moscow. German front companies including ER Industriebedarf GmbH and Amtech Solutions helped obscure the operation. Shipments reportedly reached Russia within 5 to 10 days of leaving the EU.

Goods included microcontrollers, sensors, converters, ball bearings, oscilloscopes, and electronic components—dual-use items with both civilian and military applications. Some shipments were traced to Russian defense-linked entities and nuclear research institutes.

Internal messages cited by investigators reveal the deception: "Make it look clean. No Russian reference anywhere" and "We need a different consignee—Turkey works."
sections.whyItMatters: Despite three years of Western sanctions, Russia continues accessing European technology through intermediary states. Turkey remains a critical transit point, exposing the limits of export controls when enforcement depends on cooperation from non-aligned states.

sections.heading: Russia's Undersea Sabotage Architecture Revealed
sections.body:
RUSI analysis of classified Russian government documents reveals Moscow has developed sophisticated capabilities for undersea sabotage operations targeting critical infrastructure—communications cables, energy pipelines, and surveillance systems.

The Main Directorate for Deep-Sea Research (GUGI) operates nuclear-powered deep-sea vehicles like the Losharik, capable of operating at depths up to 6,000 meters, with newer vehicles reportedly designed for 11,000 meters. GUGI cooperates with the GRU's Special Activities Service, which specializes in covert operations including sabotage.

In 2023, Russia created the 235th Specialist Training Centre in occupied Crimea, dedicated to maritime sabotage using unmanned vehicles at great depths. The unit recruits from naval special operations forces and submarine divisions. Training includes using proxies and non-Russian citizens for plausible deniability.

Russia continues investing in these capabilities even while waging war in Ukraine—spending limited resources on infrastructure with no relevance to that conflict but essential for operations against NATO.

The seabed has become a zone of grey-zone warfare. Cable sabotage offers strategic leverage without clear attribution, creating escalation risks as states struggle to determine whether disruptions are accidental, criminal, or acts of war.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia's undersea warfare capabilities represent a strategic hedge against NATO. The investment prioritization—continuing deep-sea programs despite resource constraints in Ukraine—signals Moscow's expectations of future confrontation with the West.

sections.heading: Japan Strengthens Critical Infrastructure Cyber Defense
sections.body:
Japan's government compiled a package of measures to enhance cyber defense systems for critical infrastructure operators across 15 industry sectors, including telecommunications, finance, and medicine.

The initiative responds to rising cyberattack risks amid rapid advances in artificial intelligence, particularly US startup Anthropic's AI tool Claude Mythos, which officials cite as both a defensive opportunity and an offensive threat vector.

The package includes gathering and analyzing cyber defense intelligence for operators, developing cybersecurity experts, and fostering cooperation with other governments and AI development companies. The government will also press software vendors to resolve vulnerabilities and develop patches.

The measures were adopted at the first meeting of relevant ministries, attended by officials from the National Cybersecurity Office, National Police Agency, Financial Services Agency, Digital Agency, and Defense Ministry.
sections.whyItMatters: Major economies are racing to adapt cyber defense frameworks for the AI era. Japan's explicit reference to specific AI tools reflects growing concern that generative AI capabilities will accelerate both attack sophistication and defensive requirements.

bottomLine: Today's developments converge on a single theme: digital and autonomous technologies have become central to state power projection. Iran's threats against undersea cables demonstrate that data infrastructure now faces the same coercive targeting as energy transit routes. Meanwhile, AI-powered drones are rapidly democratizing precision strike capabilities—both Ukraine's offensive systems and US counter-drone investments show how quickly battlefield innovations spread. The German sanctions case exposes the persistent gap between Western export controls and actual enforcement, with Turkey remaining a key conduit. Russia's continued investment in undersea sabotage capabilities despite Ukraine war pressures signals strategic preparation for confrontation with NATO. The physical foundations of the digital world—cables, chips, and code—are now contested terrain.
sources: Kyiv Post, Politico, Navy Times, Forbes, RUSI, Nippon, Time, Ars Technica, The Conversation, TeleGeography, 247WallSt
